behaviors.description: Generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type)
behaviors.justification: Real failure: the service generates code in a format the signature parser doesn't handle, or extracts the wrong signature. Could a pure in-process mock miss this? Yes — if you mock LLM output without realistic code formatting, you miss parser edge cases. Must test signature extraction against realistic code samples (or mock LLM returns that reflect actual LLM output formats).
behaviors.test_description: Mock LLM to return generated code with signature variations (correct parameters/types, missing parameters, type mismatches, different spacing/formatting conventions). Verify the service correctly parses and extracts the function signature, validates it matches the expected interface specification, and rejects signatures that don't conform.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration captures both the signature parsing logic and realistic code formats. Unit-level pure logic tests would miss formatting edge cases real LLMs produce.

behaviors.description: Generated implementation passes all provided test cases on first or subsequent attempt
behaviors.justification: Real failure: the LLM generates code that doesn't actually pass tests, either initially or after refinement. Would mocking LLM to always return passing code hide this failure? Yes — it would hide the core failure mode (LLM generates buggy code). The behavior asserts the implementation must work; to verify this, you need the LLM to actually attempt generating correct code, not just mock infrastructure that passes tests by design.
behaviors.test_description: Call the service with a function description that typically requires iterative refinement (e.g., an algorithm with edge cases that single LLM calls typically fail on). Verify that through one or more LLM-generated attempts, guided by test failures, the final generated implementation passes all provided test cases. Document which attempt succeeded.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic is required because correctness depends on what a real LLM produces. Integration-level mocking of LLM would let you test the refinement framework but not whether the service actually produces working code.

behaviors.description: Refinement step includes failure messages in next LLM prompt so model can learn from mistakes
behaviors.justification: Real failure: the service captures test failures but doesn't pass them to the next LLM call, or strips them from the prompt. Could a pure mock miss this? Only if the mock doesn't inspect the prompt. Must test that the exact failure messages flow from test execution → prompt construction → next LLM invocation. Requires mocking LLM and intercepting the call to verify prompt contents.
behaviors.test_description: Mock LLM and test runner such that initial attempt produces failures. On second invocation, inspect the exact prompt passed to LLM and verify it includes all failure messages from the first attempt (error type, assertion failures, stack traces). Verify message format is preserved and not truncated or corrupted.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration correctly captures the cross-component data flow (test failure → prompt inclusion). Pure Unit test of prompt-building logic alone wouldn't verify the failures actually get captured from test execution.

behaviors.description: Service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns structured error with all attempts and test results
behaviors.justification: Real failure: service makes 4+ LLM calls, or error doesn't include test results from each attempt. Could a Unit mock (pure logic test with mocked LLM + test runner) catch this? Partially — you could verify retry count logic. But would it catch missing test results? Only if you actually test that test results are captured and included, which requires realistic test execution (not just mocked return values). Integration-level testing ensures test results flow from execution into the error response.
behaviors.test_description: Mock LLM to always generate code that fails tests. Run the service on any description and verify: (1) exactly 3 LLM calls are made, (2) no 4th call occurs, (3) returned error is structured (e.g., JSON with 'attempts' array), (4) error includes all three attempts with their test results (pass/fail status and output/failure messages for each).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration ensures test results are actually captured from test execution and included in error. Unit mocking alone might verify retry logic but miss data flow failures.

behaviors.description: Full loop (generate → test → refine → test) produces working implementation for functions that single LLM call typically fails on
behaviors.justification: Real failure: the LLM generates code, refinement loop runs, but final code still doesn't pass tests (e.g., LLM doesn't actually learn from failures, or refinement doesn't improve code). This requires multiple agentic steps in sequence: call LLM → run tests → conditionally refine based on results → repeat. Each step depends on LLM output and test results; the loop only works if refinement actually improves code across multiple iterations.
behaviors.test_description: Call the service with a function description known to require iterative refinement (e.g., a function with edge cases or complex logic that single LLM calls typically fail on). Verify the service executes the full loop: generates code → runs tests → if failing, refines with failure context → tests again, repeating up to 3 times. Verify the final implementation passes all tests, confirming the refinement loop produces measurable improvement.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Workflow correctly captures the multi-step agentic loop. Single Agentic test would only verify one LLM call. The behavior is about the *loop* producing working code, which requires testing multiple sequential LLM invocations and their collective effect.
